summary: Jeff Atwood documents his frustrating experience trying to flash a BIOS, which still requires bootable DOS disks via floppy drive in 2005. He argues that the PC industry's stubborn attachment to the 1.44MB floppy drive is absurd, especially given that Apple abandoned it seven years earlier with the original iMac. While alternatives like bootable CDs and USB flash drives exist, each comes with its own complications and inconsistent BIOS support. The post serves as both a practical guide to USB boot drives and a broader critique of the PC industry's excessive backwards compatibility.
key_points: The floppy drive was never reliable or useful, yet the PC industry continues to include it on every motherboard in 2005
Apple proved floppy drives were dispensable with the 1998 iMac, yet the PC world couldn't follow suit seven years later
Bootable CDs ironically still require floppy disk images, perpetuating the legacy dependency
USB flash drives are the true successor to floppies but suffer from inconsistent BIOS support
Backwards compatibility in the PC industry comes at the cost of user sanity and progress
money_quotes: If Apple can drop the floppy from the 1998 iMac, why can't the PC industry kill this pernicious thing off seven years later?
The 'depends on the phase of the moon, brand of media, and which computer it was formatted on' unreliability
I guess the price we pay for all this glorious backwards compatibility is sanity.
Don't even get me started on PS/2 keyboard and mouse ports.
key_insight: The PC industry's obsession with backwards compatibility perpetuates obsolete technology long past its usefulness, creating unnecessary friction for users who must work around legacy constraints.
